chunk_summary: Guidelines for thiamine supplementation and lab monitoring during the first 72 hours of refeeding in pediatric GI disorders to prevent refeeding syndrome complications.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of thiamine supplementation in at-risk pediatric patients during refeeding.
Learn the recommended lab monitoring protocols for the first 72 hours of refeeding, including frequency and specific tests.
Recognize signs of biochemical refeeding syndrome and appropriate corrective actions.
Develop a written calorie advancement plan to prevent unintended calorie jumps during handovers.
key_facts: Thiamine dosage: 1–2 mg/kg/day (maximum 100 mg/day) for 5 days, starting before calorie escalation.
Day 0 lab tests: serum electrolytes (potassium, magnesium, phosphate) and blood glucose; avoid panels omitting phosphate/magnesium.
High-risk monitoring: urea/electrolytes, metabolic panel, and blood sugar every 6 hours for 24–48 hours after starting parenteral nutrition.
Lower-risk enteral starts: daily electrolyte checks are sufficient but require a written monitoring plan.
Biochemical refeeding signs: falling phosphate, potassium, magnesium, tachycardia, weakness, ileus, or respiratory symptoms.
reference_quote: Thiamine should be prescribed in at-risk children because carbohydrate metabolism increases abruptly during refeeding and can unmask deficiency. (Royal Children’s Hospital Melbourne)
document_key_topics: Calorie advancement planning in pediatric GI disorders
